food_subgroup: Mixed fish and meat rice bowl (donburi)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for teriyaki salmon and beef donburi; closest parent 'rice bowl food product' (FOODON_00002809) used as anchor.
ontology.foodex2_description: Fish and meat mixed dish with rice
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for teriyaki salmon and beef donburi; A0CKP (mixed fish and meat dish with rice) used as closest parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for this specific mixed teriyaki rice bowl dish.

main_ingredients: teriyaki sauce (soy sauce, mirin, sake, sugar)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 550 g, comprising a bowl of steamed Japanese short-grain rice topped with teriyaki-glazed salmon fillet and beef slices, accompanied by a half boiled egg and miso soup.

nutrient_source.title: Nutrient values estimated from USDA FoodData Central component foods: Salmon, Atlantic, farmed, cooked (FDC ID 175168); Beef, ground, 85% lean, pan-browned (FDC ID 174036); White rice, cooked, short-grain (FDC ID 169756); Teriyaki sauce (FDC ID 172237); Egg, whole, hard-boiled (FDC ID 173424). Weighted composite per 100 g of assembled bowl.
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for teriyaki salmon and beef donburi as a composite dish. Values are estimated by weighted-average composition of component foods from USDA FoodData Central Foundation Foods and SR Legacy entries. Assumed bowl composition per 550 g serving: steamed white rice 220 g (40%), salmon fillet cooked 130 g (24%), beef cooked 100 g (18%), teriyaki sauce 50 g (9%), half boiled egg 40 g (7%), miso soup broth 10 g (2%). Per-100 g values derived from this weighted composite.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; GI of white rice (Japanese short-grain, cooked) reported as approximately 54–56 (low); composite bowl GI estimated using carbohydrate-weighted average of rice (GI 54), teriyaki sauce (GI ~65 due to sugar content), and protein-dominant components (salmon, beef, egg — negligible GI contribution).
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021. Am J Clin Nutr. 2021;114(5):1625-1632.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for teriyaki salmon and beef donburi as a composite dish. GI estimated using carbohydrate-weighted average method: Japanese short-grain white rice (GI 54, University of Sydney GI Database) contributes ~85% of glycemic carbohydrates; teriyaki sauce sugar contribution (~15%) estimated GI 65. Protein and fat from salmon, beef, and egg attenuate postprandial glucose response. Composite GI estimated at 54 (low). Glycemic load per 550 g serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (54 × 96.25) / 100 ≈ 52.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from teriyaki sauce and miso soup
hypertension.guidance: Each 550 g serving provides approximately 1705 mg sodium, representing 74–85% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sauce options or limit portion size to manage sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index driven by white rice and teriyaki sauce sugar content, but high glycemic load due to portion size
type2_diabetes.guidance: The bowl has a low GI of approximately 54, but a high glycemic load (~52 per serving) due to the large rice portion; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider reducing the rice portion, substituting brown rice if available, and pairing with the protein components to blunt postprandial glucose excursions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Omega-3 fatty acids from salmon offset moderate saturated fat from beef
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Salmon provides cardioprotective long-chain omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA), which support favourable lipid profiles; however, the teriyaki sauce contributes significant sodium (~1705 mg per serving) that warrants caution in individuals with established cardiovascular disease or heart failure.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Teriyaki Salmon and Beef Bowl (照り焼きサーモン＆ビーフ丼) is a Japanese donburi-style rice bowl dish featuring two protein sources — teriyaki-glazed salmon fillet and thinly sliced teriyaki beef — served over a generous portion of steamed Japanese short-grain white rice. The dish is typically accompanied by a half boiled egg (ajitsuke tamago style or plain), and miso soup or a beverage. The teriyaki glaze is prepared from a classic combination of soy sauce, mirin, sake, and sugar, imparting a sweet-savoury lacquered coating to both the salmon and beef. The salmon contributes a rich, buttery texture and a mild oceanic flavour, while the beef adds a deeper umami savoriness. The rice base is soft and slightly sticky, characteristic of Japanese short-grain varieties. Nutritionally, the bowl is a high-protein, moderate-carbohydrate meal providing approximately 10.8 g protein and 17.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g. Salmon is a notable source of long-chain omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA), vitamin D, vitamin B12, and selenium, all of which are clinically relevant for cardiovascular and neurological health. The primary nutritional concern is sodium, as teriyaki sauce and miso soup together contribute substantial sodium (~1705 mg per 550 g serving), which is relevant for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 54 (low), driven primarily by the white rice component. This dish is popular in Japanese casual dining chains across Singapore, including Sukiya, and represents a balanced but sodium-rich meal suitable for the general population in moderate portions.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a teriyaki salmon and beef bowl?
Is teriyaki salmon bowl good for diabetics?
Is teriyaki bowl high in sodium?
Can I eat teriyaki salmon bowl with high blood pressure?
Glycemic index of Japanese rice bowl

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this composite dish as served commercially. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component foods using a weighted-average composition model based on plausible bowl assembly proportions. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database using the carbohydrate-weighted average method anchored to Japanese short-grain white rice. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level data sources are high quality and the estimation methodology is defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central Foundation Foods and SR Legacy entries for individual components (salmon, beef, white rice, teriyaki sauce, egg). No single approved-source entry for this exact dish exists. 2. Serving size of 550 g is an estimate based on typical Japanese donburi bowl portions as described in the restaurant context; actual restaurant portion may vary. 3. Sodium estimate (~310 mg per 100 g; ~1705 mg per serving) is likely conservative given commercial teriyaki sauce and miso soup sodium levels; actual sodium may be higher. 4. GI of 54 is an analogous estimate anchored to Japanese short-grain white rice from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this composite dish was found in approved sources. 5. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA component data and are not individually confirmed from a single source entry; these values carry medium uncertainty. 6. Halal status is not confirmed; teriyaki sauce may contain mirin (alcohol) and the beef sourcing is not specified, so is_halal is set to false pending confirmation. 7. FoodOn URI FOODON_00002809 (rice bowl food product) is used as closest parent; no exact FoodOn term for teriyaki salmon and beef donburi was found. FoodEx2 code A0CKP is used as closest parent for mixed fish and meat dish with rice. 8. Input food_name '974. Teriyaki Salmon Bowl with Beef Combo' has been canonicalised to 'Teriyaki Salmon and Beef Bowl' by removing the menu item number and 'Combo' suffix.

improvement_actions_taken: Corrected `gi_category` from 'medium' to 'low' as glycemic_index 54 falls into the 'low' GI category (<=55) per University of Sydney GI Database guidelines.
Updated `gi_source.source_match_notes` to reflect 'Composite GI estimated at 54 (low)'.
Updated `health_context.type2_diabetes.key_factor` and `guidance` to refer to 'low GI' instead of 'medium GI' for consistency with the corrected gi_category.
